steps.title: Welcome to Day 6
steps.body: One of the best things about AI is that it does not get bored, does not take feedback personally, and can critique your work in seconds. Today you will learn how to use it as a sharp, honest editor.

steps.title: What AI can critique
steps.body: AI can review almost any type of work: emails, presentations, business plans, speeches, applications, reports, proposals, or anything you write. It can find weak spots, missing logic, and unclear phrasing — fast.

steps.title: The key: ask for specific feedback
steps.body: Vague request: "What do you think of this?" Useful request: "Find three weaknesses in this argument" or "What would someone who disagrees say?" Specifics turn a general review into something you can act on.

steps.title: Before: a weak feedback request
steps.prompt: Can you look at my proposal and tell me if it is good?

steps.title: After: a strong feedback prompt
steps.prompt: Act as a tough but fair critic. Here is my project proposal: [paste text]. 1. What are the three weakest points in my argument? 2. What questions would a skeptical reader have? 3. What is missing that would make this more convincing? Be direct.

steps.title: What changed
steps.body: The stronger prompt: named a role (tough but fair critic), provided the content, asked specific numbered questions, and said "be direct." Each of those details shapes a more useful, actionable response.

steps.title: Quick check
steps.question: You want AI to improve your cover letter. Which prompt will give the most useful feedback?
options: Act as a hiring manager. Here is my cover letter: [paste]. What are the top three things that would make this stronger? Also, what would make you stop reading?
Tell me if I will get the job.
feedback: This will get a vague yes or no — not useful.
Excellent. A role, the content, specific questions, and a practical angle make this highly actionable.
AI can fix surface issues, but without knowing what to focus on, it may miss the important gaps.
AI cannot predict hiring decisions — only tell you how the letter reads.

steps.title: True or false?
steps.question: You should only show AI finished, polished work to get useful feedback.
feedback: Early drafts often get the most useful feedback. AI can spot structural problems before you spend time polishing something that needs rebuilding.
Right. Rough drafts are perfect for feedback — better to find problems early than after a lot of work.

steps.title: Good fit?
steps.question: Act as a devil's advocate. Here is my plan to leave my job and freelance full-time: [paste plan]. What are the biggest risks I have not thought about?
feedback: Yes — asking AI to challenge your thinking is one of its most valuable uses. It helps you prepare for what you have not considered.
Actually this is an excellent use of AI. Getting a critical perspective before a major decision is exactly what it is good for.

steps.title: Good fit?
steps.question: Tell me whether my business will succeed.
feedback: AI cannot predict business success — too many factors are outside its view. But it can help you stress-test your plan, find gaps, and think through risks.
Right. A better approach: ask AI to find weaknesses in your plan, not to predict the outcome.

steps.title: True or false?
steps.question: Asking AI to "steelman" an argument means asking it to find the strongest possible version of your idea.
feedback: Actually, steelmanning means presenting the strongest form of an argument — AI can do this to help you see your idea at its best before you defend it.
Right. "Steelman my argument" asks AI to make your idea as strong as possible — useful before a presentation or pitch.

steps.title: Build a feedback prompt

fields.label: What work are you asking AI to review?
fields.placeholder: e.g. a short speech I am giving at my company meeting

fields.label: What specific feedback do you want?
fields.placeholder: e.g. is the main point clear, does it flow well, what is weak

fields.label: What kind of critic should AI be?
fields.placeholder: e.g. a tough but constructive editor

steps.template: Act as {{voice}}. Here is my {{work}}: [paste your work here]. Please give me feedback on: {{focus}}. Be specific and direct.

steps.title: Copy your feedback prompt
steps.body: Copy this prompt, add your actual work in place of [paste your work here], and send it to your AI tool.

steps.title: Paste the feedback you received
steps.body: Paste the critique AI gave you here so we can save it to your work.

questions.label: Did AI point out anything you had not noticed yourself?
options: No — I had already seen all of it

questions.label: What is one specific improvement you will make based on the feedback?

steps.title: Match the feedback goal to the prompt

pairs.right: What questions would a skeptical reader have?
Act as a devil's advocate and challenge this

questions.question: What makes a feedback prompt most useful?
options: Keeping the request open-ended so AI can choose what to fix
Asking specific questions and giving AI the actual content to review
Only showing AI work that is already nearly perfect

questions.question: Asking AI to 'be direct' in its feedback will make the response more actionable.
To get AI to challenge your plan, ask it to act as a ______ advocate.

steps.title: Day 6 complete
steps.body: You now know how to use AI to critique, strengthen, and stress-test your work — before anyone else sees it. That is a powerful editing loop. One more day to go — see you tomorrow.
